WORKS

PUBLISHED BY

ARCHIBALD CONSTABLE & CO. EDINBURGH,

AND SOLD BY

JOHN MURRAY, 32. FLEET-STREET, LONDON.

I.

THE FARMER'S MAGAZINE, a Periodical Work, exclusively devoted to Agricultural and Rural Affairs, 1800, 1801, 1802, 1803, 1804, 1805. 6 Vol. boards. 21. 14s.; or, in Numbers, price 21. 11s. Published Quarterly.

¶ The design of the Farmer's Magazine is to collect and disseminate ingenious Theories, important and well-authenticated Facts, and accurate Experiments, which relate to the different branches of rural economy. It is well known to those acquainted with the different parts of the United Kingdom, that discoveries and improvements in Agriculture travel very slowly. To remedy this, the Farmer's Magazine was first projected; and if the Proprietors may judge from the almost unprecedented sale of more than 4000 copies of each Number, the object of this publication is in a great degree answered. The Work consists of—1. Miscellaneous Communications of the most important practical nature.—2. The Review of Agricultural Publications.—3. Agricultural Intelligence from every district in Scotland and England. The importance of this branch must be evident to every practical Farmer, who, at the moderate price of Two Shillings and Sixpence a quarter, may learn the state of the crops, and the price of grain, cattle, &c. in the different parts of the kingdom, besides being enabled to compare his own practice with that in other districts, and thus to correct what is improper or deficient.

II.

THE EDINBURGH REVIEW, or CRITICAL JOURNAL, from its commencement in October 1802, to January 1806. Published Quarterly. Handsomely printed in Seven Volumes Octavo, price 31. 13s. 6d. in boards, or in 14 Numbers, Price 5s. each.

¶ This Publication is conducted upon a principle of Selection: It takes no notice of insignificant works, but enters very fully into the discussion of those that seem entitled to attention. Combining, in some measure, the representative functions of a Review with the independence of original discussion; comprehending every foreign Publication which an extensive Continental correspondence can procure; and distinguished by an impartiality which no party zeal has yet pretended to call in question—it lays claim to the support of those who are not satisfied with the indolent and indiscriminating profusion of our other Journals.

The Editors were silent upon these pretensions, till they saw how the Public was likely to receive them. Their success has been much beyond their expectation, and, they believe, beyond any former example. In spite of the size of their Volume, the remoteness of their situation, and all the disadvantages of inexperience, they were enabled, even in their Second Number, to equal the circulation of several of the established Journals of the metropolis; and they have been convinced, by the con-

stant increase of the demand, that, to secure the patronage of the Public, it is only necessary to deserve it by a diligent and conscientious discharge of their duty. To every Number is annexed a complete List of all the new Publications in the preceding quarter. This Catalogue will contain by far the earliest and most comprehensive account of modern Literature that has yet been presented to the Public.

III.

EDINBURGH MEDICAL and SURGICAL JOURNAL, No. I. II. III. & IV. for the year 1805. Exhibiting a concise View of the latest and most important Discoveries in Medicine, Surgery and Pharmacy. Published quarterly. Handsomely printed in 8vo. Price 12s. 6d. boards, or 12s. in Numbers. Volume First.

¶ The object of the EDINBURGH MEDICAL and SURGICAL JOURNAL, is the improvement of Medicine, by collecting the scattered hints, and registering the important facts connected with Medical Science and Medical Practice.

After the experience of a year, the Editors have the satisfaction to find their labours rewarded by the approbation of the Public. In the departments allotted to Original Communications, and to Miscellaneous Intelligence on Medical Subjects, the respectability of the assistance they have received, has surpassed even their most sanguine expectations; and they have every reason to believe that it will increase with the increasing size of their Work. In the present volume are contained communications from Professors Monro junior, Pechoni, Rutherford, and Stringham; Drs Baillie, Barclay, Bateman, Bostock, Cullen, Clancy, De Rocher, Drew, Duncan junior, Inglis, Kellie, Lubbock, M'Gregor, Marcet, R. Pearson, Reeve, Robinson, Ross, Spens, and Wood; and Messrs Barlow, Carmichael, Coates, Collins, Asley Cooper, Dalrymple, Fabbrou, Livingstone, Lucas, M'Mullin, Robertson, and Rumsey; besides articles from other Correspondents, whose names would add to the reputation of the Journal, if the Editors were at liberty to divulge them. That portion of their work appropriated to Medical Literature, they have endeavoured to render interesting and instructive, by combining impartial analysis with original disquisition. The extent of their Continental connections, and the number of valuable publications at home, will at all times supply them with such an ample store of materials, that their principal difficulty will consist in selection.

The authors of British publications analyzed, are, Sir J. Banks, Bart. Drs Adams, Bostock, Brown, Cox, Currie, Harrison, Henderson, Haygarth, Kinglake, Kirby, and Trotter; Messrs Abernethy, Carlisle, Edlin, Home, Whately, and Wilkinson; and of foreign publications, M. M. Barthez, Celler, Dupuy, Foderé, Görcke, Mahon, Mounier, Prost, Roth, Scarpa, Soemmering, Tavares, and Willdenow; besides the works of several respectable Medical Societies and Public Institutions, both foreign and domestic.

D. Wilson, printer, Edinburgh.]

WORKS PUBLISHED BY ARCHIBALD CONSTABLE & CO.
IV.

THE SCOTS MAGAZINE, and EDINBURGH LITERARY MISCELLANY; being a General Repository of Literature, History, and Politics, for 1805. Vol. LXVII. Price 18s. in 12 Numbers, or 1l. neatly half-bound.

V.

A COMPLETE SET OF THE SCOTS MAGAZINE, from its commencement in 1739 to 1805 inclus. 67 vol. 37l.

¶ The Scots Magazine, and Edinburgh Literary Miscellany, is published regularly on the first day of each month. It consists of Original Correspondence on various subjects—Memoirs and Anecdotes of distinguished characters, particularly Scotsmen—Scottish Antiquities—Accounts of Improvements and New Institutions—Manners of Nations—Literary Intelligence—With an account of New Discoveries in the Arts and Sciences, and a copious Monthly Register, which may be confidently referred to as an authentic and valuable record of the public, as well as of every important domestic occurrence of the day. Each Number is embellished with an engraved View of some remarkable place in Scotland.

VI.

TRAVELS TO DISCOVER THE SOURCE OF THE NILE, in the years 1768, 1769, 1770, 1771, 1772, and 1773. By JAMES BRUCE of Kinnaid, Esq. F. R. S. The Second Edition, corrected and enlarged. To which is prefixed a Life of the Author. Handsomely printed in 7 volumes 8vo. with a Volume of Copperplates in 4to. Price 4l. 16s. bds.—¶ A few copies are printed in Royal Octavo, with full impressions of the Plates. Price 7l. 7s. boards.

* * This Edition of a highly celebrated work, is printed from a correct copy, intended for the press at the time of the Author's death. The use of all his Original Journals, Correspondence, Manuscripts, &c.; the concurring testimonies of later Travellers; along with various other circumstances, have enabled the Editor to render the work more complete and interesting than Mr Bruce himself would have condescended to make it.—To the Account of his Life, which occupies a considerable part of the First Volume, is annexed a selection of Letters from a very valuable Correspondence, in which the names of the Comte de Bassin, the Hon. Dalnes Barrington, Lord Halifax, Mr Wood, Dr Hugh Blair, Mr Andrew Lumsden, Sir Robert Strange, Dr Solander, and many others, deserve particular notice.

The Copperplates, which are all engraved by HEATH, are upwards of Eighty in number, and include various specimens of Natural History, Portraits of celebrated Abyssinians, and other interesting subjects, not in the former edition, and never before given to the public.

VII.

AN INQUIRY INTO THE NATURE and ORIGIN OF PUBLIC WEALTH, and into the Means and Causes of its Increase. By the EARL of LAUDERDALE. 1 vol. 8vo. 8s. 6d. bds.

VIII.

OBSERVATIONS, by the EARL of LAUDERDALE, on the Review of his Inquiry into the Nature and Origin of Public Wealth, published in the VIIIth Number of the Edinburgh Review. Price 2s.

IX.

THOUGHTS on the ALARMING STATE of the CIRCULATION, and of the Means of Redressing Pecuniary Grievances in Ireland. By the EARL of LAUDERDALE. 8vo. 3s. 6d. fewed.

X.

HINTS to the MANUFACTURERS of GREAT BRITAIN on the Consequences of the Irish Union, and the System

once pursued, of Borrowing in England, for the Service of Ireland. By the EARL of LAUDERDALE. Price 1s. 6d.

XI.

AN ESSAY on NAVAL TACTICS, Systematical and Historical, with explanatory Plates. By JOHN CLERK of Eldin, Esq. Second Edition. One Volume 3to. 1l. 16s. boards.

¶ In contemplating the beautiful simplicity and unqualified efficacy of Mr Clerk's system, it is peculiarly pleasing to reflect, that it is directed upon principles not less congenial than honourable to the character of the nation for whose use it was intended. This system does not consist in any trick or manoeuvre, by which courage may be rendered unnecessary, or gallantry decoyed to its destruction; on the contrary, it proceeds upon the proud presumption, that we shall certainly beat our enemies if we can only get near enough to grapple with them; and its sole object is to give the valiant an opportunity of safe fighting, to counteract the shifting policy of a wily adversary, and to insure a fair field for the display of courage, discipline, and perseverance in strenuous and decisive contest.—Edinburgh Review, No. XII. p. 312.

XII.

ELEMENTS of MECHANICAL PHILOSOPHY, being the Substance of a Course of Lectures on that Science. By JOHN ROBISON, LL.D. Professor of Natural Philosophy in the University of Edinburgh. One very large Volume 8vo. with 22 Copperplates, 1l. 1s. boards.

XIII.

A COLLECTION of MATHEMATICAL TABLES, for the use of the Practical Mathematician, Navigator, Surveyor, Students in Universities, and for men of business. By ANDREW MACKAY, LL.D. F. R. S. Ed. &c. In 1 vol. 8vo. Price 7s. in boards.

XIV.

THE COMPLETE NAVIGATOR; or, an easy and familiar Guide to the Theory and Practice of Navigation, with all the requisite Tables, &c. &c. By ANDREW MACKAY, LL.D. F. R. S. Author of the Theory and Practice of finding the Longitude at Sea or Land, &c. In 1 large vol. 8vo. illustrated with Engravings, &c. Price 10s. 6d. bound.

* * The Utility of the Art of Navigation to every Commercial and Maritime State is obvious; but to none more so, than to Britain; and, as the lives and fortunes of many depend upon the accuracy of the Rules and Tables given in the books published on this subject, the greatest care imaginable ought, therefore, to be taken, in order to have them correct. This is not the case, however, with some of the most popular books; of which a few instances are pointed out in the preface to the present work, and which was one of the principal reasons for embarking in this undertaking. The Author has spared neither labour nor expense to render it deserving of the public attention, and as perfect as possible.

XV.

TRACTS ON THE NATURAL HISTORY of ANIMALS and VEGETABLES. Second Edition, greatly enlarged. Translated from the Original Italian of the Abbé Spallanzani, Royal Professor of Natural History in the University of Pavia, F. R. S. London, &c. &c. By JOHN GRAHAM DALYELL, Esq. With Illustrations, by the Translator. In Two large Vols. 8vo. illustrated by Eleven Copperplates. Price 15s. boards.

* * The acknowledged fame of the celebrated Author of these Tracts, and the importance of his numerous discoveries, are too well established to require

page 7 · 192775020.3.jpg
WORKS PUBLISHED BY ARCHIBALD CONSTABLE & CO.
3

require any additional confirmation here. His name has justly been enrolled with that of the most illustrious European physiologists; and his works will ever remain an emblem of genius and research. All bear indisputable evidence of profound investigation into the phenomena of nature, and of the most comprehensive and enlightened understanding. The Treatises on the Natural History of Animals and Vegetables will rank with the first of his productions; for the new, singular, and interesting matter they contain, will be an equal source of pleasure and admiration to the philosophic reader.

XVI.

The EXPERIENCED MILL-WRIGHT, or a Treatise on the Construction of some of the most useful Machines, with the latest Discoveries; to which is prefixed, a short Account of the General Principles of Mechanics, and the Mechanical Powers. By ANDREW GRAY, Mill-Wright. One Volume Imperial 4to. 11. 16s. boards.

XVII.

An INQUIRY into the COLONIAL POLICY of the EUROPEAN POWERS. By HENRY BROUGHAM, jun. Esq. F.R.S. 2 vol. 8vo. 18s. boards.

XVIII.

The HISTORY of EGYPT, from the earliest Accounts of that Country, till the Expulsion of the French from Alexandria in the year 1801. By JAMES WILSON, D. D. Minister of Falkirk. With a Map. 3 vol. 8vo. 11. 4s. boards.

XIX.

The HISTORY of SCOTLAND, from the Union of the Crowns on the Accession of James VI. to the throne of England, to the Union of the Kingdoms in the Reign of Queen Anne. With a Historical and Critical Dissertation on the supposed authenticity of Ossian's Poems. Second Edition, corrected. To which is prefixed, a Dissertation on the Participation of Mary Queen of Scots in the Murder of Darnley. By MALCOLM LAING, Esq. 4 vol. 8vo. 11. 16s. boards.

XX.

The HISTORIE and LIFE of KING JAMES SIXTH, written towards the latter end of the 16th century. Published from an original MS. By MALCOLM LAING, Esq. 8vo. 10s. boards.

XXI.

The HISTORY of the ORKNEY ISLANDS, in which is comprehended, an Account of their Present as well as their Ancient State; together with the Advantages they possess for several Branches of Industry; and the Means by which they may be improved. Illustrated with an accurate and extensive Map of the whole Islands, and with Plates of some of the most interesting Objects they contain. By the Reverend GEORGE BARRY, D. D. Minister of Shapinshay. 4to. boards, 11. 12s. 6d.

XXII.

The POEMS of OSSIAN, containing the Poetical Works of James Macpherson, Esq. in Prose and Rhyme: with Notes and Illustrations. By MALCOLM LAING, Esq. 2 Vol. 8vo. 11. 10s. boards.

XXIII.

REPORT of the COMMITTEE of the HIGHLAND SOCIETY of SCOTLAND, appointed to inquire into the Nature and Authenticity of the Poems of Ossian. Drawn up, according to the Direction of the Committee, by HENRY MACKENZIE, Esq. their Convener and Chairman. With a copious Appendix, containing some of the principal Documents on which the Report is founded. Illustrated by three curious engraved Specimens of ancient Gaelic MSS. 8vo. 12s. boards.

XXIV.

PRIZE ESSAYS and TRANSACTIONS of the HIGHLAND SOCIETY of SCOTLAND. 2 vols. 8vo. 16s. boards.

N. B. Volume II. may be had separately.

XXV.

OBSERVATIONS on the PRESENT STATE of the HIGHLANDS of SCOTLAND; with a View of the Causes and probable Consequences of Emigration. By the EARL of SELKIRK. Second Edition. 8vo. 6s. boards.

XXVI.

The MINSTRELSY of the SCOTISH BORDER: consisting of Historical and Romantic Ballads, collected in the Southern Counties of Scotland: with a few of Modern Date, founded on Local Tradition. With an Introduction and Notes by the Editor, WALTER SCOTT, Esq. Advocate. In 3 vol. 8vo. Price 11. 11s. 6d. boards.

XXVII.

SIR TRISTREM, a Romance, by Thomas of Erleddoun. Published from the Auchinleck MS. in the Advocates' Library. With a preliminary Dissertation and Glossary, by WALTER SCOTT, Esq. Advocate. Beautifully printed by Ballantyne. One Volume Royal 8vo. 21. 2s. boards. (Only 150 Copies printed.)

XXVIII.

The LAY of the LAST MINSTREL, a Poem. By WALTER SCOTT, Esq. Advocate. Third Edition. 8vo. Price 10s. 6d. boards.

XXIX.

A CHRONICLE of SCOTISH POETRY, from the Thirteenth Century to the Union of the Crowns: with Notes. To which is added, a Glossary, intended to serve as a Dictionary of the ancient Language of Scotland. By JAMES SIMBALD. 4 vol. Crown 8vo. 11. 4s. boards.

XXX.

The POETICAL WORKS of HECTOR MACNEIL, Esq. Elegantly printed by Bensley. In 2 vols. Ornamented with Nine descriptive Plates, and a Portrait of the Author.

XXXI.

FRAGMENTS of SCOTISH HISTORY, containing the Diary of Robert Birrel, 1532—1608.—An Account of the

WORKS PUBLISHED BY ARCHBALD CONSTABLE & CO.

the Expedition into Scotland under the Earl of Hertford, 1544.—Patten's Account of the Expedition into Scotland under the Duke of Somerset, 1547. Illustrated with Three Plans, representing the Battle of Pinkie. 410. Price 1l. 1s. boards.

XXXII.

SCOTISH POEMS of the Sixteenth Century; containing, Ane Booke of Godly and Spirituall Songs—The Lamentations of Lady Scotland—The Testament and Tragedie of unquhile King Henry Stewart, 1567—Ane Declarations of the Lords just Quarrel, 1567—Grange's Ballat, 1571—The Seige of the Castle of Edinburgh, 1575—The Legend of a Bisshop, callit Mr. Patrick Adamson—The Battell of Balrines, in anno 1594. To which, in illustration, are prefixed, 1. Curfory Remarks. 2. Some Incidents in the Life of Regent Murray. 3. Biographical Sketches of Sir William Kirkaldy of Grange. 4. A faithful Narrative of the Battle of Balrines, and a Glossary. 2 vol. 12mo. 12s. boards.

XXXIII.

BIOGRAPHIA SCOTICA; or, SCOTISH BIOGRAPHICAL DICTIONARY, containing a short Account of the Lives and Writings of the most Eminent Persons, and Remarkable Characters, Natives of Scotland, from the earliest ages to the present time. By J. STARK. Embellished with Portraits. In one vol. 12mo. Price 5s.

XXXIV.

The GAZETTEER of SCOTLAND; containing a particular Description of the Counties, Cities, Rivers, Mountains, &c. &c. &c. &c. Illustrated with an elegant Map. In one Vol. 8vo. Price 1os. 6d. boards.

XXXV.

The SCHOLAR'S VADE-MECUM, or a new DICTIONARY, Latin and English. Consisting of such words as are purely classical, in which the English is given from the best authorities, and the Latin words explained in all their variations, according to the most approved Grammars. For the use of Schools. The Third Edition, much improved. By JAMES MOIR, Teacher of Languages. In One Volume Crown 8vo. 5s. bound.

* * * Though this book was originally intended for the Tyro, yet it may be of considerable advantage to the more advanced Student, who, it is more than probable, has forgot his grammar rules, as he will here more readily find a solution of any difficulty, than by turning over his grammar, especially as it has no Index; and he can depend upon the clearness of nouns and verbs in this, whereas the larger Dictionaries will, for the most part, mislead him.

XXXVI.

An ABRIDGEMENT of all the STATUTES now in force relative to the Revenue of Excise in Great Britain, methodically arranged, and alphabetically digested. The Second Edition, revised and brought down to the end of the year 1803. By JAMES HUIS, Collector of Excise. 8vo. 1os. 6d. boards.

XXXVII.

GENERAL VIEW of the AGRICULTURE of the COUNTY of PEEBLES. With various Suggestions as to the Means, both of the Local and General Improvement of Agriculture. By the Reverend CHARLES FINDLATER, Newlands. 8vo. 7s. boards.

XXXVIII.

The GENTLEMAN and FARMER'S POCKET COMPANION and ASSISTANT, consisting of Tables for finding the Contents of any Piece of Land, by Pacing, or by Dimensions taken on the Spot in Ells. By JOHN AINSLIE, Land-Surveyor. 5s. bound.

XXXIX.

SERMONS by WILLIAM LAWRENCE BROWN, D. D. Principal of Marischal College and University, Professor of Divinity, and Minister of Grey-Friar's Church, Aberdeen, one of his Majesty's Chaplains in Ordinary in Scotland, and Member of the Provincial Societies of Arts and Sciences of Utrecht and Holland. In 1 vol. 8vo. Price 7s. in boards.

XL.

The WORKS of JOSEPH BUTLER, D. D. late Lord Bishop of Durham, with a Preface, &c. by SAMUEL HALIFAX, D. D. late Lord Bishop of Gloucester. 2 vol. 8vo., beautifully printed by BALLANTYNE. Price 18s. boards.

* * * The Publishers of this Edition of the Works of this eminent Divine, beg leave to call the attention of the Public to the complete form in which they are now, for the first time, prefixed. Besides his celebrated Treatise upon the Anatomy of Religion, and the Sermons delivered by him at the Rolls Chapel, and other places, these volumes contain Bishop Butler's Correspondence with Dr. Clarke on the Attributes of the Deity, and every other production of that profound writer which he has furnished by the authority of his name. In short, the Publishers have been anxious to prefer, in an elegant and uniform shape, every memorial of a name which must at all times be regarded with admiration by the scholar, and with reverence by the Christian. Prefixed to the whole is a life of B. B. by Dr. Kippis.

XLI.

A Treatise entitled, THOUGHTS on PUBLIC TRUSTS. From which it appears, that the Integrity and Patriotism of the Rulers in Ancient Rome, and the rapacity of the Rulers in Modern France, were inevitable consequences of the respective Constitutions of these Nations. 12mo. Price 2s. 6d. boards.

XLII.

ELEMENTS of INTELLECTUAL PHILOSOPHY, or an Analysis of the POWRS of the HUMAN UNDERSTANDING, Tending to ascertain the Principles of a Rational Logic. By R. E. SCOTT, A. M. Professor of Moral Philosophy in the University and King's College, Aberdeen. One Volume 8vo. 9s. boards.

XLIII.

PICTURE of EDINBURGH, containing a History and Description of the City—an Account of its Antiquities, Political, Civil, and Municipal Establishments—Literary Establishments,

page 9 · 192775046.3.jpg
WORKS PUBLISHED BY ARCHIBALD CONSTABLE & CO.
5

ments, including a history of the progress and present state of Printing, of Literature, and of the Arts—Religious Establishments—Charitable Institutions—Public Amusements, including a History of the Theatre, Music, and Dancing—Progress and present state of Manners—Markets, Water, and Fuel—History and Description of Leith—Population—Objects of Natural History in the immediate neighbourhood of Edinburgh—and a Description of the Romantic Scenery and Remarkable Objects in the environs of the Scottish Metropolis. By J. STARK. Illustrated with a Plan, and upwards of thirty Wooden Cuts. Very handsomely printed. Price 6s. boards.

XLIV.

In Two sheets—Price One Guinea, neatly mounted on Canvas and Rollers, a beautiful and highly finished MAP of the City of EDINBURGH, LEITH, and ENVIRONS, most accurately done from actual Surveys and Measurements. By JOHN AINSLIE, Geographer. The whole Etched and Engraved by himself.

* * * This Map comprehends the whole of the newly projected Streets and Squares, &c. with the new Entrances and Approaches to the Town, by the different new roads, and extends over the New Extended Royalty at Belvedere, &c. &c.—with the Port of Leith—Newhaven—the new Wet Docks there; and, in short, every place of importance connected with, or in the immediate vicinity of the Metropolis.

The grounds belonging to the different proprietors are shaded with different colours, so that they are readily distinguished from each other. And the different scales of measurement will be found to be constructed with such accuracy, that, by the application of them, a portion of an acre even may be positively ascertained.

XLV.

A SHORT STATEMENT of some IMPORTANT FACTS, relative to the late Election of a Mathematical Professor in the University of Edinburgh; accompanied with Original Papers and Critical Remarks. By Professor DUGALD STEWART. Third edition. 8vo. Price 2s. 6d. fewed.

XLVI.

POSTSCRIPT to Mr STEWART'S SHORT STATEMENT of FACTS, relative to the Election of Professor Leslie; with an Appendix, consisting chiefly of Extracts from the Records of the University, and from those of the City of Edinburgh. 8vo. Price 1s.

XLVII.

LETTER to the AUTHOR of the EXAMINATION of Professor STEWART'S SHORT STATEMENT of FACTS. With an Appendix. By JOHN PLAYFAIR, A. M. Professor of Mathematics in the University of Edinburgh. 2s.

XLVIII.

REPORT of the PROCEEDINGS and DEBATE in the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland, respecting the Election of Mr Leslie to the Mathematical Chair in the University of Edinburgh. Neatly printed in 8vo. Second Edition.

* * * Upon the whole, to preserve a faithful picture of a Debate, in every point of view interesting and important, is the object of the present publication. We have not exaggerated, but, on the contrary, could we have done so conscientiously with truth, we should have been inclined to soften some of its harsher features; and there are those who know how anxiously we have studied to exclude any thing which might hurt the feelings of individuals, when that could be accomplished without affecting the impartiality of the Work.

XLIX.

A TREATISE on the ORIGIN and QUALITIES and CULTIVATION of MOSS-EARTH. By WILLIAM AITKEN, Writer, Strathaven. 8vo. Price 3s. fewed.

* * * The above Work, besides being warmly recommended by the Highland Society of Scotland, as meriting in a high degree the attention of their members, and every practical farmer throughout the kingdom, has been most favourably noticed by the Literary Journalists of the day.

The acute and intelligent author of the present Treatise appears to have directed his mind, with much ardour, to collect information, and make observations, on the nature and improvement of Moss Earth. Thinking it of great importance to rouse the attention of his countrymen to so great an advantage, and thinking the observations he himself had to communicate, might be useful to direct the efforts of many who might be disposed to concern in the undertaking, he was induced to offer his performance to the public. Judging of objects by their apparent utility, and not by the brilliancy of their form, or the magnitude of their pretensions, we are disposed to reckon this modest Treatise among the most important things which have come into our hands.—London Literary Journal for July 1805.

* * * See also the Farmer's Magazine, No. 25.

L.

ELEMENTS of MATERIA MEDICA and PHARMACY. By JOHN MURRAY, Lecturer on Chemistry, Materia Medica, and Pharmacy. Two Volumes 8vo. 14s. boards.

LI.

A SYSTEM of MINERALOGY; Comprehending Oryctognosie, Geognosie, Mineralogical Chemistry, Mineralogical Geography, and Economical Mineralogy. By ROBERT JAMESON, Regius Professor of Natural History, and Keeper of the Museum in the University of Edinburgh, &c. Volume First. Handsomely Printed in Octavo, Illustrated by Eleven Engravings. Price 14s. boards.

PHARMACOPÆIA NOSOCOMII REGII EDINBURGENSIS. Foolscap 8vo. Price 3s. boards.

OBSERVATIONS on the UTILITY and ADMINISTRATION of PURGATIVE MEDICINES, in Several Diseases. By JAMES HAMILTON, M.D. Fellow of the Royal College of Physicians, and of the Royal Philosophical Society; and Senior Physician to the Royal Infirmary, Edinburgh. 8vo. Price 6s. boards.

LIV.

The PRINCIPLES and PRACTICE of AGRICULTURE, Systematically explained; being a Treatise Compiled for the Fourth Edition of the Encyclopædia Britannica, and Revised and Enlarged. By ROBERT FORSYTH, Esq. 8vo. Price 21s. boards.

LVI.

An INTRODUCTION to MERCHANDIZE; Containing a Complete System of Arithmetic. A System of Algebra. Forms and Manner of transacting Bills of Exchange. Bookkeeping in Various Forms.
